Our evaluation across 11 GPU types from four generations of major architectures on two widely-used serving systems demonstrates that SyncPerf delivers high fidelity and strong generalizability. It achieves accurate predictions, with only 6.1% average error at the kernel level and 8.5% for end-to-end inference -- reducing the error of state-of-the-art methods by 6.7x and 4.4x, respectively. We also demonstrate SynPerf's value "beyond simulation" by utilizing its performance ceiling to diagnose implementation shortcomings and guide the optimization of a production fused MoE Triton kernel, achieving up to 1.7x speedup.
memory.why_now: GPU choice and inference optimization are immediate operational concerns for anyone serving LLMs, and this paper claims unusually strong cross-GPU prediction accuracy on production-style serving workloads. The MoE kernel optimization example makes it more relevant than a pure modeling paper because it connects prediction to actionable systems tuning.
memory.why_not_higher: The memory/storage angle is indirect: this is mainly a GPU performance-modeling paper, not a KV cache, offload, paging, or bandwidth-management paper. It also risks becoming a niche systems episode unless the discussion is framed around practical serving decisions rather than the predictor itself.
downgrade_reasons: Memory link is adjacent rather than core
Broad-audience appeal is moderate, not obvious breakout interest
Could land as a benchmarking/modeling episode rather than a research-shift episode
memory.what_would_raise_priority: Clear evidence that teams can use SynPerf to make better real deployment choices across rapidly changing LLM inference stacks would raise it.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if you could predict how an LLM inference stack will run on a new GPU before doing the full port, and use that model to find the next 1.7x kernel win?

memory.title: From Servers to Sites: Compositional Power Trace Generation of LLM Inference for Infrastructure Planning
memory.abstract: Datacenter operators and electrical utilities rely on power traces at different spatiotemporal scales. Operators use fine-grained traces for provisioning, facility management, and scheduling, while utilities use site-level load profiles for capacity and interconnection planning. Existing datacenter power models do not capture LLM inference workloads, in which GPUs shift rapidly among compute-intensive prefill, lower-power decode, and idle states, and facility demand depends on how these states evolve and synchronize across many devices. We show that LLM inference power can be represented compositionally through two components: workload-driven transitions among operating states and configuration-specific power distributions within those states. Building on this observation, we develop a trace-generation framework that learns from measured traces and synthesizes power profiles for new traffic conditions and serving configurations. These traces aggregate from GPU servers to rack-, row-, and facility-scale load profiles at the temporal granularity required by the study.   Across multiple LLMs, tensor-parallel settings, and GPU generations, our framework achieves median absolute energy error below 5% for most configurations while preserving temporal autocorrelation structure. The resulting traces support downstream analyses including oversubscription, power modulation, and utility-facing load characterization, enabling infrastructure evaluations that flat nameplate assumptions and static trace replay cannot support.
memory.why_now: LLM inference is becoming a real datacenter and grid-planning problem, and this paper tackles a practical gap: generating realistic power traces instead of relying on flat nameplate assumptions. That matters now because large-scale serving deployments are pushing operators and utilities to plan around bursty, state-dependent GPU demand.
memory.why_not_higher: The contribution is infrastructure planning rather than a change to mainstream model building or serving practice for most AI teams. Its memory/storage relevance is weak, and the audience is narrower than papers about latency, cost, KV cache, or throughput bottlenecks.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage link is adjacent at best, not central
Primary impact is datacenter power planning, a niche systems topic
Does not obviously change day-to-day LLM serving design for most practitioners
memory.what_would_raise_priority: Evidence that these traces materially improve real serving decisions such as scheduler design, rack oversubscription policy, or cost/performance optimization would raise it.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the next LLM bottleneck is not tokens per second but whether your datacenter and local utility can predict the power swings of inference at scale?

monitor.title: MemEvolve: Meta-Evolution of Agent Memory Systems
monitor.abstract: Self-evolving memory systems are unprecedentedly reshaping the evolutionary paradigm of large language model (LLM)-based agents. Prior work has predominantly relied on manually engineered memory architectures to store trajectories, distill experience, and synthesize reusable tools, enabling agents to evolve on the fly within environment interactions. However, this paradigm is fundamentally constrained by the staticity of the memory system itself: while memory facilitates agent-level evolving, the underlying memory architecture cannot be meta-adapted to diverse task contexts. To address this gap, we propose MemEvolve, a meta-evolutionary framework that jointly evolves agents' experiential knowledge and their memory architecture, allowing agent systems not only to accumulate experience but also to progressively refine how they learn from it. To ground MemEvolve in prior research and foster openness in future self-evolving systems, we introduce EvolveLab, a unified self-evolving memory codebase that distills twelve representative memory systems into a modular design space (encode, store, retrieve, manage), providing both a standardized implementation substrate and a fair experimental arena. Extensive evaluations on four challenging agentic benchmarks demonstrate that MemEvolve achieves (I) substantial performance gains, improving frameworks such as SmolAgent and Flash-Searcher by up to $17.06\%$; and (II) strong cross-task and cross-LLM generalization, designing memory architectures that transfer effectively across diverse benchmarks and backbone models.
monitor.why_now: Agent memory is an active theme, and this paper tries to move beyond hand-designed memory modules by searching over the memory architecture itself. That makes it timely for people tracking long-horizon agents and agent engineering patterns.
monitor.why_not_higher: The "memory" here is mostly agent experience management, not memory/storage systems in the podcast's core sense of KV cache, paging, offload, bandwidth, or serving behavior. The gains are benchmark-driven and interesting, but they do not yet clearly change how mainstream AI systems are trained or served.
downgrade_reasons: Memory connection is agent-memory adjacent rather than systems-memory core
Evidence is benchmark-centric with no concrete hardware or serving implications
May read as an agent framework optimization rather than a broader AI systems shift
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: A clearer demonstration that the evolved memory designs generalize into widely adopted agent stacks or produce concrete retrieval, latency, or context-efficiency gains in production-like settings would raise priority.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if AI agents did not just learn from experience, but also evolved the very memory architecture they use to remember?

monitor.title: Ask a Strong LLM Judge when Your Reward Model is Uncertain
monitor.abstract: Reward model (RM) plays a pivotal role in reinforcement learning with human feedback (RLHF) for aligning large language models (LLMs). However, classical RMs trained on human preferences are vulnerable to reward hacking and generalize poorly to out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs. By contrast, strong LLM judges equipped with reasoning capabilities demonstrate superior generalization, even without additional training, but incur significantly higher inference costs, limiting their applicability in online RLHF. In this work, we propose an uncertainty-based routing framework that efficiently complements a fast RM with a strong but costly LLM judge. Our approach formulates advantage estimation in policy gradient (PG) methods as pairwise preference classification, enabling principled uncertainty quantification to guide routing. Uncertain pairs are forwarded to the LLM judge, while confident ones are evaluated by the RM. Experiments on RM benchmarks demonstrate that our uncertainty-based routing strategy significantly outperforms random judge calling at the same cost, and downstream alignment results showcase its effectiveness in improving online RLHF.
monitor.why_now: Labs are actively trying to reduce alignment and evaluation costs while keeping strong judge quality, and this paper targets that exact operational bottleneck in online RLHF. The routing idea also fits the current shift toward hybrid systems that selectively call expensive reasoning models only when needed.
monitor.why_not_higher: The memory/storage link is essentially absent, and the main contribution is a cost-quality routing strategy inside RLHF rather than a broader systems change. It also reads more like a solid alignment/benchmark paper than a paper that will immediately reshape mainstream model building or serving.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Primarily an RLHF routing result rather than a broader systems advance
Episode risks collapsing into alignment benchmark details
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Clear evidence that this routing pattern improves production-scale post-training or generalizes beyond the specific RLHF setup would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the right way to scale RLHF is not a better reward model, but knowing exactly when to hand uncertain decisions to a stronger LLM judge?

monitor.title: Stable and Efficient Single-Rollout RL for Multimodal Reasoning
monitor.abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has become a key paradigm to improve the reasoning capabilities of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). However, prevalent group-based algorithms such as GRPO require multi-rollout sampling for each prompt. While more efficient single-rollout variants have recently been explored in text-only settings, we find that they suffer from severe instability in multimodal contexts, often leading to training collapse. To address this training efficiency-stability trade-off, we introduce $\textbf{MSSR}$ (Multimodal Stabilized Single-Rollout), a group-free RLVR framework that achieves both stable optimization and effective multimodal reasoning performance. MSSR achieves this via an entropy-based advantage-shaping mechanism that adaptively regularizes advantage magnitudes, preventing collapse and maintaining training stability. While such mechanisms have been used in group-based RLVR, we show that in the multimodal single-rollout setting they are not merely beneficial but essential for stability. In in-distribution evaluations, MSSR demonstrates superior training compute efficiency, achieving similar validation accuracy to the group-based baseline with half the training steps. When trained for the same number of steps, MSSR's performance surpasses the group-based baseline and shows consistent generalization improvements across five diverse reasoning-intensive benchmarks. Together, these results demonstrate that MSSR enables stable, compute-efficient, and effective RLVR for complex multimodal reasoning tasks.
monitor.why_now: Efficient RL for reasoning-capable multimodal models is a live topic, and a stable single-rollout alternative to group-based RLVR is relevant to teams trying to cut training cost. If the result holds beyond the reported setup, it points to a more practical recipe for post-training multimodal reasoners.
monitor.why_not_higher: The memory/storage link is effectively absent: this is a training-stability and RL optimization paper, not a cache, bandwidth, offload, or serving paper. It also looks more like a benchmarked method improvement inside the RLVR niche than a broadly validated shift in how most AI systems are built.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory or storage mechanism
Benchmark-heavy evidence rather than deployment evidence
Primarily relevant to multimodal RLVR training pipelines
Unclear impact on mainstream serving or inference stacks
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Stronger cross-model, cross-dataset, and production-scale evidence showing the method reliably replaces group-based RLVR in widely used multimodal post-training pipelines would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can multimodal reasoning models get the gains of RL without the cost and instability of multi-rollout training?

monitor.title: MAS-Orchestra: Understanding and Improving Multi-Agent Reasoning Through Holistic Orchestration and Controlled Benchmarks
monitor.abstract: While multi-agent systems (MAS) promise elevated intelligence through coordination of agents, current approaches to automatic MAS design under-deliver. Such shortcomings stem from two key factors: (1) methodological complexity - agent orchestration is performed using sequential, code-level execution that limits global system-level holistic reasoning and scales poorly with agent complexity - and (2) efficacy uncertainty - MAS are deployed without understanding if there are tangible benefits compared to single-agent systems (SAS). We propose MASOrchestra, a training-time framework that formulates MAS orchestration as a function-calling reinforcement learning problem with holistic orchestration, generating an entire MAS at once. In MAS-Orchestra, complex, goal-oriented subagents are abstracted as callable functions, enabling global reasoning over system structure while hiding internal execution details. To rigorously study when and why MAS are beneficial, we introduce MASBENCH, a controlled benchmark that characterizes tasks along five axes: Depth, Horizon, Breadth, Parallel, and Robustness. Our analysis reveals that MAS gains depend critically on task structure, verification protocols, and the capabilities of both orchestrator and subagents, rather than holding universally. Guided by these insights, MAS-Orchestra achieves consistent improvements on public benchmarks including mathematical reasoning, multi-hop QA, and search-based QA, while achieving more than 10x efficiency over strong baselines. Together, MAS-Orchestra and MASBENCH enable better training and understanding of MAS in the pursuit of multi-agent intelligence.
monitor.why_now: Multi-agent reasoning is still a live topic, and this paper is more useful than most because it tries to separate real gains from orchestration hype with a controlled benchmark. The combination of a trainable orchestration framework and task-axis analysis makes it timely for teams experimenting with agent systems.
monitor.why_not_higher: The memory/storage connection is effectively absent, and the broad AI value depends on whether MASBench actually predicts gains on messy real deployments rather than benchmarked workflows. Multi-agent papers are also crowded right now, so a new one needs clearer operational impact to force immediate coverage.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Benchmark-heavy evidence with limited real-world deployment proof
Multi-agent gains appear conditional rather than broadly decisive
Episode risks becoming another agents-evaluation discussion unless framed carefully
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Stronger evidence that the orchestration method improves production-grade agent workflows on realistic long-horizon tasks with clear cost, latency, and reliability gains would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Most multi-agent systems add complexity first and value later, so this episode asks whether we can finally measure when a swarm of LLM agents beats one strong model and when it is just orchestration theater.

monitor.title: BugPilot: Complex Bug Generation for Efficient Learning of SWE Skills
monitor.abstract: High quality bugs are key to training the next generation of language model based software engineering (SWE) agents. We introduce a novel method for synthetic generation of difficult and diverse bugs. Our method instructs SWE Agents to introduce a feature into the codebase whereby they may unintentionally break tests, resulting in bugs. Prior approaches often induce an out-of-distribution effect by generating bugs intentionally (e.g. by introducing local perturbation to existing code), which does not reflect realistic development processes. We perform qualitative analysis to demonstrate that our approach for generating bugs more closely reflects the patterns found in human-authored edits. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that our bugs provide more efficient training data for supervised fine-tuning, outperforming other bug datasets by 2% with half the training data (1.2k vs. 3k bugs). We train on our newly generated bugs in addition to existing bug datasets to get FrogBoss a state-of-the-art 32B parameter model on SWE-bench Verified with a pass@1 of 54.6% and FrogMini a state-of-the-art 14B model on SWE-bench Verified with a pass@1 of 45.3% on SWE-bench Verified averaged over three seeds.
monitor.why_now: SWE agents are an active frontier, and this paper targets a real bottleneck: producing training bugs that look more like human development mistakes rather than synthetic perturbations. If the gains hold, it suggests better data generation may move coding-agent performance more efficiently than just scaling dataset size.
monitor.why_not_higher: The contribution is still centered on SWE-bench-style bug fixing, which is important but narrower than methods that broadly change LLM training or serving. It also has no meaningful memory/storage mechanism, so it only fits one editorial lens.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage link is absent
Primarily a SWE-agent training paper
Benchmark gains may matter more to agent builders than the broader AI audience
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Stronger evidence that the bug-generation method transfers beyond SWE-bench to broader coding-agent training and real developer workflows would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the fastest way to build better coding agents is not bigger models, but teaching them on bugs that fail like real software does?

monitor.title: LLM-CAS: Dynamic Neuron Perturbation for Real-Time Hallucination Correction
monitor.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) often generate hallucinated content that lacks factual or contextual grounding, limiting their reliability in critical applications. Existing approaches such as supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning from human feedback are data intensive and computationally expensive, while static parameter editing methods struggle with context dependent errors and catastrophic forgetting.   We propose LLM-CAS, a framework that formulates real-time hallucination correction as a hierarchical reinforcement learning problem. LLM-CAS trains an agent to learn a policy that dynamically selects temporary neuron perturbations during inference based on the current context. Unlike prior dynamic approaches that rely on heuristic or predefined adjustments, this policy driven mechanism enables adaptive and fine grained correction without permanent parameter modification.   Experiments across multiple language models demonstrate that LLM-CAS consistently improves factual accuracy, achieving gains of 10.98 percentage points on StoryCloze, 2.71 points on TriviaQA, and 2.06 points on the MC1 score of TruthfulQA. These results outperform both static editing methods such as ITI and CAA and the dynamic SADI framework. Overall, LLM-CAS provides an efficient and context aware solution for improving the reliability of LLMs, with promising potential for future multimodal extensions.
monitor.why_now: Hallucination correction remains a live concern for anyone deploying LLMs, and a context-dependent inference-time intervention is more relevant to current product teams than another costly retraining recipe. The paper also fits the ongoing search for lightweight reliability methods that do not permanently rewrite model weights.
monitor.why_not_higher: The memory/storage link is effectively absent, and the contribution looks like a specialized control method for factuality rather than a broader shift in how modern AI systems are built or served. The evidence is benchmark-centric, with limited indication of production realism, latency cost, or behavior on larger deployment settings.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage connection is absent rather than central
Primarily a hallucination-correction niche instead of a broadly transferable systems result
Evaluation appears benchmark-driven with limited deployment realism
Unclear inference overhead and operational complexity of neuron perturbation policy
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Stronger real-world serving evidence, including latency/throughput tradeoffs on production-scale models and broader gains across open-ended factual generation tasks, would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can you fix LLM hallucinations on the fly by nudging the right neurons at inference time, without retraining or permanently editing the model?

monitor.title: Patterning: The Dual of Interpretability
monitor.abstract: Mechanistic interpretability aims to understand how neural networks generalize beyond their training data by reverse-engineering their internal structures. We introduce patterning as the dual problem: given a desired form of generalization, determine what training data produces it. Our approach is based on susceptibilities, which measure how posterior expectation values of observables respond to infinitesimal shifts in the data distribution. Inverting this linear response relationship yields the data intervention that steers the model toward a target internal configuration. We demonstrate patterning in a small language model, showing that re-weighting training data along principal susceptibility directions can accelerate or delay the formation of structure, such as the induction circuit. In a synthetic parentheses balancing task where multiple algorithms achieve perfect training accuracy, we show that patterning can select which algorithm the model learns by targeting the local learning coefficient of each solution. These results establish that the same mathematical framework used to read internal structure can be inverted to write it.
monitor.why_now: Mechanistic interpretability remains a live topic, and this paper flips the usual question by asking how to shape training data to induce desired internal circuits. That inversion is conceptually fresh and could matter to people thinking about controllable training dynamics and representation formation.
monitor.why_not_higher: The evidence appears to come from a small language model and a synthetic task, so it does not yet show impact on realistic frontier-scale training or serving. The memory/storage link is not direct, and the practical path from susceptibilities to widely used training recipes is still speculative.
downgrade_reasons: memory connection is absent rather than systems-central
validation is on small-scale and synthetic settings
unclear near-term effect on production model building
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: A stronger priority case would come from showing the method steers useful behaviors in modern LLM pretraining or finetuning at meaningful scale.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if interpretability could be run in reverse, letting us design the training data that writes specific circuits into a model instead of just discovering them afterward?

monitor.title: DeepWideSearch: Benchmarking Depth and Width in Agentic Information Seeking
monitor.abstract: Current search agents fundamentally lack the ability to simultaneously perform \textit{deep} reasoning over multi-hop retrieval and \textit{wide}-scale information collection-a critical deficiency for real-world applications like comprehensive market analysis and business development. To bridge this gap, we introduce DeepWideSearch, the first benchmark explicitly designed to evaluate agents to integrate depth and width in information seeking. In DeepWideSearch, agents must process a large volume of data, each requiring deep reasoning over multi-hop retrieval paths. Specifically, we propose two methods to converse established datasets, resulting in a curated collection of 220 questions spanning 15 diverse domains. Extensive experiments demonstrate that even state-of-the-art agents achieve only 2.39% average success rate on DeepWideSearch, highlighting the substantial challenge of integrating depth and width search in information-seeking tasks. Furthermore, our error analysis reveals four failure modes: lack of reflection, overreliance on internal knowledge, insufficient retrieval, and context overflow-exposing key limitations in current agent architectures. We publicly release DeepWideSearch to catalyze future research on more capable and robust information-seeking agents.
monitor.why_now: Agentic search and tool-using LLMs are a live topic, and this benchmark targets a real weakness: combining broad evidence gathering with multi-hop reasoning under context pressure. The extremely low success rates make it a useful snapshot of where current agents still break.
monitor.why_not_higher: This is mainly an evaluation benchmark, not a method that changes how people build or serve systems today. Its memory connection is indirect, with context overflow appearing as a failure mode rather than a concrete memory/cache/storage mechanism under study.
downgrade_reasons: benchmark-only contribution
memory link is incidental rather than central
limited direct systems or deployment takeaway
realism of tasks is curated rather than production-grounded
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: A follow-up that demonstrates concrete agent architecture fixes on this benchmark, especially with retrieval, context management, or long-context systems tradeoffs, would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Why do top search agents collapse when they need to think deeply across lots of evidence at once, and what does that reveal about the limits of today's agent stack?

monitor.title: Bottom-up Policy Optimization: Your Language Model Policy Secretly Contains Internal Policies
monitor.abstract: Existing reinforcement learning (RL) approaches treat large language models (LLMs) as a unified policy, overlooking their internal mechanisms. In this paper, we decompose the LLM-based policy into Internal Layer Policies and Internal Modular Policies via Transformer's residual stream. Our entropy analysis on internal policy reveals distinct patterns: (1) universally, policies evolve from high-entropy exploration in early layers to deterministic refinement in top layers; and (2) Qwen exhibits a progressive, human-like reasoning structure, contrasting with the abrupt final-layer convergence in Llama. Furthermore, we discover that optimizing internal layers induces feature refinement, forcing lower layers to capture high-level reasoning representations early. Motivated by these findings, we propose Bottom-up Policy Optimization (BuPO), a novel RL paradigm that reconstructs the LLM's reasoning foundation from the bottom up by optimizing internal layers in early stages. Extensive experiments on complex reasoning benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of BuPO. Our code is available at https://github.com/Trae1ounG/BuPO.
monitor.why_now: It reframes LLM RL as optimization over internal layer policies rather than a single monolithic policy, which is a live topic as reasoning-focused post-training keeps expanding. The layerwise entropy analysis and bottom-up training recipe make it potentially relevant to how people think about training reasoning-capable models.
monitor.why_not_higher: The memory/storage link is effectively absent: this is about internal policy structure and RL optimization, not cache, bandwidth, offload, paging, or data movement behavior. It also reads more like a method-plus-benchmark paper than a proven change to production model training practice.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage connection is absent rather than direct
Evidence appears benchmark-centric rather than deployment-centric
Unclear impact on real-world LLM training pipelines at scale
Reasoning/RL papers are crowded, so novelty must clear a higher bar
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Show clear gains on frontier-scale post-training workloads with convincing ablations and evidence that bottom-up optimization changes practical LLM training recipes beyond benchmark improvements.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if an LLM is not one policy at all, but a stack of hidden policies that should be trained from the bottom up?

monitor.title: CudaForge: An Agent Framework with Hardware Feedback for CUDA Kernel Optimization
monitor.abstract: Developing efficient CUDA kernels is increasingly critical for AI applications such as large-scale LLM training. However, manual kernel design is both costly and time-consuming, motivating automatic approaches that leverage LLMs for code generation. Existing methods for automatic kernel generation, however, often produce low-efficiency kernels, incur high computational overhead, and fail to generalize across settings. In this work, we propose CudaForge, a training-free multi-agent workflow for CUDA kernel generation and optimization. Our workflow is inspired by the iterative workflow of human experts, which contains steps such as developing initial kernels, testing correctness, analyzing hardware feedback, and iterative improvement. More specifically, CudaForge employs two LLM agents: a Coder and a Judge, that iteratively generate, correct, and optimize CUDA kernels, while integrating hardware feedback such as Nsight Compute (NCU) metrics. In extensive evaluations, we show that CudaForge, by leveraging base models like OpenAI-o3, achieves 97.6\% correctness of generated kernels and an average 1.68$\times$ speedup over PyTorch baselines, substantially surpassing state-of-the-art models including OpenAI-o3 and Kevin on KernelBench.Beyond accuracy and speed, CudaForge demonstrates strong generalization across GPUs (A100, RTX 6000, 4090, 3090) and base models (OpenAI-o3, GPT-5, gpt-oss-120B, Claude-Sonnet-4, QwQ-32B), while maintaining high efficiency. In particular, generating an optimized kernel takes about 26.5 minutes on one RTX6000 and incurs about \$ 0.3 API cost, which is significantly cheaper than existing agentic work that costs 6 H100 hours and \$ 5 API cost per kernel. Our results highlight that multi-agent, training-free workflows can enable cost-effective, generalizable, and high-performance CUDA kernel optimization. Code available at https://github.com/OptimAI-Lab/CudaForge
monitor.why_now: LLM-driven code generation is moving from demos toward real systems work, and this paper claims a cheaper, more reliable agent loop for CUDA optimization using hardware feedback. That is timely for teams trying to squeeze more performance out of training and inference kernels without hand-tuning everything.
monitor.why_not_higher: The memory/storage link is mostly indirect: this is a kernel optimization framework, not a paper about KV cache, offload, paging, tiering, or bandwidth management as a first-class mechanism. The evaluation is stronger than many agent papers, but it is still centered on KernelBench-style kernels rather than end-to-end model serving or training outcomes.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage mechanism is adjacent rather than central
Kernel-benchmark focus limits direct relevance to most listeners
Unclear how much it changes real end-to-end AI system design today
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Show consistent end-to-end gains on widely used training or inference workloads and make memory behavior a clearer part of the optimization story.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can a pair of LLM agents, guided by Nsight hardware counters, turn CUDA kernel tuning from expert black art into a cheap, repeatable optimization loop?

monitor.title: Recontextualization Mitigates Specification Gaming without Modifying the Specification
monitor.abstract: Developers often struggle to specify correct training labels and rewards. Perhaps they don't need to. We propose recontextualization, which reduces how often language models "game" training signals, performing misbehaviors those signals mistakenly reinforce. We show recontextualization prevents models from learning to 1) prioritize evaluation metrics over chat response quality; 2) special-case code to pass incorrect tests; 3) overwrite evaluation functions rather than write correct code; and 4) become sycophantic. Our method works by generating completions from prompts discouraging misbehavior and then recontextualizing them as though they were in response to prompts permitting misbehavior. Recontextualization trains language models to resist misbehavior even when instructions permit it. This mitigates the reinforcement of misbehavior from misspecified training signals, reducing specification gaming without improving the supervision signal.
monitor.why_now: Specification gaming and reward misspecification are active problems for post-training and evaluation of modern LLMs. A method that claims to reduce gaming without fixing labels or rewards is timely because labs are scaling imperfect human and automated feedback pipelines right now.
monitor.why_not_higher: The memory/storage connection is absent, so it only competes on the public-interest lens. From the abstract, the evidence looks promising but still centered on controlled failure modes rather than clearly established deployment-scale training workloads.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Evidence appears mostly task-level rather than production-scale
Practical adoption cost and robustness across model families are still unclear
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Stronger evidence on large post-training pipelines or frontier-style eval setups, with clear robustness across models and misspecification types, would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if you could make language models stop gaming bad rewards without ever fixing the bad reward in the first place?

monitor.title: ICPO: Illocution-Calibrated Policy Optimization for Multi-Turn Conversation
monitor.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) in multi-turn conversations often suffer from a ``lost-in-conversation'' phenomenon, where they struggle to recover from early incorrect assumptions, particularly when users provide ambiguous initial instructions. We find that standard post-training techniques like Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) exacerbate this issue by rewarding confident, direct answers, thereby inducing overconfidence and discouraging the model from seeking clarification. To address this, we propose Illocution-Calibrated Policy Optimization (ICPO), a novel training framework that sensitizes the model to instruction ambiguity. ICPO augments the training corpus with underspecified prompts and conditions the reward signal on the user's illocutionary intent, rewarding the model for expressing uncertainty or asking for clarification when faced with ambiguity. Experiments demonstrate that ICPO fosters appropriate humility, yielding a substantial average improvement of 75\% in multi-turn conversation, while preserving robust performance on single-turn benchmarks. Our work presents a practical path toward more robust and collaborative conversational AI that can better navigate the nuances of human interaction.
monitor.why_now: Multi-turn assistant failure recovery and clarification behavior are live product problems for chat systems, agents, and customer-facing copilots. A training method that rewards asking clarifying questions instead of bluffing fits current concerns about overconfidence in deployed LLMs.
monitor.why_not_higher: The paper appears centered on conversational behavior tuning rather than a broader shift in model architecture, serving, or systems design. Its memory/storage relevance is absent, and the reported gains may depend heavily on benchmark construction around ambiguity.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Conversation-specific post-training paper
Unclear realism of workloads and deployment settings
Closer to behavior benchmarking than systems change
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Stronger evidence on production-style agent or assistant workloads, with clear gains across widely used models and evaluation settings, would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Why do chatbots double down on bad assumptions, and can we train them to stop, ask, and recover before the conversation derails?

monitor.title: Weight Decay may matter more than muP for Learning Rate Transfer in Practice
monitor.abstract: Transferring the optimal learning rate from small to large neural networks can enable efficient training at scales where hyperparameter tuning is otherwise prohibitively expensive. To this end, the Maximal Update Parameterization (muP) proposes a learning rate scaling designed to keep the update dynamics of internal representations stable across different model widths. However, the scaling rules of muP rely on strong assumptions, particularly about the geometric alignment of a layer's inputs with both its weights and gradient updates. In this large-scale empirical investigation, we show that these assumptions hold only briefly at the start of training in the practical setups where learning rate transfer is most valuable, such as LLM training. For the remainder of training it is weight decay rather than muP that correctly stabilizes the update dynamics of internal representations across widths, facilitating learning rate transfer. This suggests muP's scaling primarily acts as a form of implicit learning rate warmup, allowing us to largely replace it with modified warmup schedules. Together these findings fundamentally challenge prevailing beliefs about learning rate transfer and can explain empirical observations such as why muP requires the independent weight decay variant for good transfer.
monitor.why_now: Large-model training teams still care about reliable learning-rate transfer, and this paper directly challenges a widely cited scaling story around muP. If the result holds, it points practitioners toward simpler warmup and weight-decay choices instead of a heavier parameterization change.
monitor.why_not_higher: The paper is important mainly to people working on optimizer and scaling recipes, not the broader AI audience. It also has no direct memory or storage mechanism, so it does not fit the podcast's second editorial lens.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage link is absent
Primarily an optimizer and training-recipe paper
Audience is narrower than core public-interest papers
Practical impact depends on replication across training stacks
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Clear evidence that major LLM training pipelines can replace muP with simpler schedules and reliably save tuning time at production scale would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if one of the best-known ideas for learning-rate transfer in large models mostly works because it accidentally behaves like warmup, while weight decay is doing the real heavy lifting?

monitor.title: Imbalanced Gradients in RL Post-Training of Multi-Task LLMs
monitor.abstract: Multi-task post-training of large language models (LLMs) is typically performed by mixing datasets from different tasks and optimizing them jointly. This approach implicitly assumes that all tasks contribute gradients of similar magnitudes; when this assumption fails, optimization becomes biased toward large-gradient tasks. In this paper, however, we show that this assumption fails in RL post-training: certain tasks produce significantly larger gradients, thus biasing updates toward those tasks. Such gradient imbalance would be justified only if larger gradients implied larger learning gains on the tasks (i.e., larger performance improvements) -- but we find this is not true. Large-gradient tasks can achieve similar or even much lower learning gains than small-gradient ones. Further analyses reveal that these gradient imbalances cannot be explained by typical training statistics such as training rewards or advantages, suggesting that they arise from the inherent differences between tasks. This cautions against naive dataset mixing and calls for future work on principled gradient-level corrections for LLMs.
monitor.why_now: RL post-training is becoming a larger part of how frontier and open LLMs are tuned, so a paper showing that naive multi-task mixing systematically biases learning is timely. The result matters because many teams assume task mixing is a harmless default when scaling post-training pipelines.
monitor.why_not_higher: The memory/storage connection is effectively absent, so it does not fit the podcast's second lens. It also appears more like a training pathology diagnosis than a concrete new recipe people can immediately deploy.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Primarily diagnostic rather than a solved systems method
Real-world impact depends on follow-on correction methods
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Show a simple gradient-level correction that consistently improves multi-task RL post-training on widely used LLMs and evaluation suites.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if multitask RL post-training is quietly letting the loudest tasks dominate LLM learning, even when they are not the ones teaching the model the most?

monitor.title: KORE: Enhancing Knowledge Injection for Large Multimodal Models via Knowledge-Oriented Augmentations and Constraints
monitor.abstract: Large Multimodal Models encode extensive factual knowledge in their pre-trained weights. However, its knowledge remains static and limited, unable to keep pace with real-world developments, which hinders continuous knowledge acquisition. Effective knowledge injection thus becomes critical, involving two goals: knowledge adaptation (injecting new knowledge) and knowledge retention (preserving old knowledge). Existing methods often struggle to learn new knowledge and suffer from catastrophic forgetting. To address this, we propose KORE, a synergistic method of KnOwledge-oRientEd augmentations and constraints for injecting new knowledge into large multimodal models while preserving old knowledge. Unlike general text or image data augmentation, KORE automatically converts individual knowledge items into structured and comprehensive knowledge to ensure that the model accurately learns new knowledge, enabling accurate adaptation. Meanwhile, KORE stores previous knowledge in the covariance matrix of LMM's linear layer activations and initializes the adapter by projecting the original weights into the matrix's null space, defining a fine-tuning direction that minimizes interference with previous knowledge, enabling powerful retention. Extensive experiments on various LMMs, including LLaVA-v1.5-7B, LLaVA-v1.5-13B, and Qwen2.5-VL-7B, show that KORE achieves superior new knowledge injection performance and effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting.
monitor.why_now: Keeping multimodal models up to date without catastrophic forgetting is a real operational problem as LMMs are pushed into longer-lived products. KORE targets that practical gap with a concrete knowledge-injection recipe tested on current open multimodal model families.
monitor.why_not_higher: The memory angle is not a systems memory/storage contribution; this is model editing and continual fine-tuning, not KV cache, offload, paging, or bandwidth work. It also reads more like a specialized knowledge-injection method than a paper that broadly changes how most teams train or serve modern AI systems.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage connection is indirect rather than systems-level
Primarily a knowledge-injection benchmark paper
Unclear how well the method transfers beyond curated update settings
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Stronger evidence on messier real-world update streams and broader transfer to production-style multimodal maintenance workflows would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can you teach a large multimodal model new facts without erasing the old ones, or are we still just doing catastrophic forgetting with nicer math?

monitor.title: Budget-Aware Tool-Use Enables Effective Agent Scaling
monitor.abstract: Scaling test-time computation improves performance across different tasks on large language models (LLMs), which has also been extended to tool-augmented agents. For these agents, scaling involves not only "thinking" in tokens but also "acting" via tool calls. The number of tool calls directly bounds the agent's interaction with the external environment. However, we find that simply granting agents a larger tool-call budget fails to improve performance, as they lack "budget awareness" and quickly hit a performance ceiling. To address this, we study how to scale such agents effectively under explicit tool-call budgets, focusing on web search agents. We first introduce the Budget Tracker, a lightweight plug-in that provides the agent with continuous budget awareness, enabling simple yet effective scaling. We further develop BATS (Budget Aware Test-time Scaling), an advanced framework that leverages this awareness to dynamically adapt its planning and verification strategy, deciding whether to "dig deeper" on a promising lead or "pivot" to new paths based on remaining resources. To analyze cost-performance scaling in a controlled manner, we formalize a unified cost metric that jointly accounts for token and tool consumption. We provide the first systematic study on budget-constrained agents, showing that budget-aware methods produce more favorable scaling curves and push the cost-performance Pareto frontier. Our work offers empirical insights toward a more transparent and principled understanding of scaling in tool-augmented agents.
monitor.why_now: Tool-using agents are a live topic, and this paper targets a real failure mode in current agent practice: bigger action budgets do not automatically yield better results. The budget-aware framing is timely because labs and product teams are actively trying to trade off reasoning depth, tool use, latency, and cost.
monitor.why_not_higher: The work appears centered on web search agents rather than a broad slice of modern AI systems, so the generality is not yet fully proven. It is also not a memory/storage paper in the editorial sense, despite being about resource budgets and cost-aware inference.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage link is absent
Evidence appears concentrated on web search agents
Practical impact outside agent evaluation loops is still uncertain
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Stronger evidence across multiple agent settings and tools, especially coding or enterprise workflows, would make the scaling claim feel more broadly consequential.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Why do bigger tool budgets stop helping AI agents, and what changes when the agent actually knows how much budget it has left?

monitor.title: OpenMMReasoner: Pushing the Frontiers for Multimodal Reasoning with an Open and General Recipe
monitor.abstract: Recent advancements in large reasoning models have fueled growing interest in extending such capabilities to multimodal domains. However, despite notable progress in visual reasoning, the lack of transparent and reproducible data curation and training strategies remains a major barrier to scalable research. In this work, we introduce OpenMMReasoner, a fully transparent two-stage recipe for multimodal reasoning spanning supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL). In the SFT stage, we construct an 874K-sample cold-start dataset with rigorous step-by-step validation, providing a strong foundation for reasoning capabilities. The subsequent RL stage leverages a 74K-sample dataset across diverse domains to further sharpen and stabilize these abilities, resulting in a more robust and efficient learning process. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that our training recipe not only surpasses strong baselines but also highlights the critical role of data quality and training design in shaping multimodal reasoning performance. Notably, our method achieves a 11.6% improvement over the Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct baseline across nine multimodal reasoning benchmarks, establishing a solid empirical foundation for future large-scale multimodal reasoning research. We open-sourced all our codes, pipeline, and data at https://github.com/EvolvingLMMs-Lab/OpenMMReasoner.
monitor.why_now: Open multimodal reasoning is a live race, and this paper offers a fully open SFT-plus-RL recipe with released data, code, and strong gains over a widely used Qwen2.5-VL-7B baseline. That makes it relevant to researchers trying to reproduce or extend multimodal reasoning systems rather than just consume closed-model results.
monitor.why_not_higher: The memory/storage link is effectively absent, so it does not serve the podcast's second lens. On the public-interest lens, it still risks reading as another benchmark-and-recipe paper unless the episode can clearly separate what is structurally new from the broader flood of reasoning work.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Primarily a training recipe plus benchmark improvement
Evidence is benchmark-centric rather than deployment/system-centric
Crowded multimodal reasoning topic area
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: A clearer demonstration that the recipe changes how open multimodal models are trained in practice across multiple base families or at larger production-relevant scales would raise priority.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the real bottleneck in multimodal reasoning progress is not secret model sauce, but whether anyone can finally reproduce the data and training recipe end to end?

monitor.title: SkyRL-Agent: Efficient RL Training for Multi-turn LLM Agent
monitor.abstract: We introduce SkyRL-Agent, a framework for efficient, multi-turn, long-horizon agent training and evaluation. It provides efficient asynchronous dispatching, lightweight tool integration, and flexible backend interoperability, enabling seamless use with existing RL frameworks such as SkyRL-train, VeRL, and Tinker.   Using SkyRL-Agent, we train SA-SWE-32B, a software engineering agent trained from Qwen3-32B (24.4% Pass@1) purely with reinforcement learning. We introduce two key components: an optimized asynchronous pipeline dispatcher that achieves a 1.55x speedup over naive asynchronous batching, and a tool-enhanced training recipe leveraging an AST-based search tool to facilitate code navigation, boost rollout Pass@K, and improve training efficiency. Together, these optimizations enable SA-SWE-32B to reach 39.4% Pass@1 on SWE-Bench Verified with more than 2x cost reduction compared to prior models reaching similar performance. Despite being trained solely on SWE tasks, SA-SWE-32B generalizes effectively to other agentic tasks, including Terminal-Bench, BrowseComp-Plus, and WebArena. We further demonstrate SkyRL-Agent's extensibility through case studies on deep research, computer use, and memory agents, each trained using a different training backend.
monitor.why_now: Agent training for long-horizon tool use is a live topic, and this paper claims meaningful cost and throughput gains while pushing a pure-RL coding agent to competitive SWE-Bench Verified performance. The infrastructure angle also matters because many groups are now bottlenecked on efficient multi-turn agent rollouts rather than base model pretraining alone.
monitor.why_not_higher: The memory/storage link is effectively absent, so it does not help the podcast's second lens. It is also still heavily anchored to SWE and agent benchmark progress, which makes it harder to separate durable systems insight from a strong task-specific training recipe.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory or storage mechanism
Crowded agent benchmark space
Evidence is strongest on coding agents rather than broadly across domains
Part of the gain may come from engineering and tooling specifics rather than broadly reusable learning principles
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Stronger cross-domain evidence showing the same training stack reliably improves non-coding agents and clearer ablations isolating which systems ideas transfer.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What does it take to train a real multi-turn coding agent efficiently enough to matter, and are better rollouts now more important than bigger models?

monitor.title: JEPA-Reasoner: Decoupling Latent Reasoning from Token Generation
monitor.abstract: Current autoregressive language models couple high-level reasoning and low-level token generation into a single sequential process, making the reasoning trajectory vulnerable to compounding expression errors. We propose JEPA-Reasoner, a novel architectural paradigm that decouples these tasks using a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) for pure latent-space reasoning and a separate Talker module for linguistic reconstruction. By isolating the reasoning engine from the discrete token-sampling process, our architecture enables: (1) Error Containment, where token-level failures cannot propagate into the latent reasoning chain; (2) Continuous Guidance, providing the generator with access to the entire lossless reasoning trajectory; and (3) Representation of Uncertainty, allowing the model to maintain multiple hypotheses via mixed latent vectors. Controlled experiments on synthetic and natural language tasks demonstrate that this decoupling enables a 0.9B model to achieve a 149.5\% improvement in 8-shot GSM8K accuracy over a coupled Transformer baseline trained on identical data. This work shifts the focus from scaling coupled models to investigating decoupled architectures as a more robust foundation for complex reasoning.
monitor.why_now: Reasoning architectures are under active scrutiny, and this paper argues for a clean break between latent reasoning and token emission rather than just scaling standard autoregressive models. That makes it timely for a broad AI audience watching the post-CoT, post-test-time-compute design space.
monitor.why_not_higher: The evidence is still early: a 0.9B model, synthetic tasks, and benchmark gains that do not yet show clear impact on production-scale training or serving. The memory/storage connection is absent, so it does not advance the podcast's second editorial lens.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Limited scale and workload realism
Unclear systems or deployment implications
Reasoning gains concentrated in benchmark-style evaluation
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: A follow-up showing robust gains on larger models and realistic reasoning workloads, with clear implications for training or inference design, would raise it substantially.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if language models should reason in latent space first and only speak afterward, instead of thinking one token at a time?

monitor.title: MobileWorld: Benchmarking Autonomous Mobile Agents in Agent-User Interactive and MCP-Augmented Environments
monitor.abstract: Among existing online mobile-use benchmarks, AndroidWorld has emerged as the dominant benchmark due to its reproducible environment and deterministic evaluation; however, recent agents achieving over 90% success rates indicate its saturation and motivate the need for a more challenging benchmark. In addition, its environment lacks key application categories, such as e-commerce and enterprise communication, and does not reflect realistic mobile-use scenarios characterized by vague user instructions and hybrid tool usage. We introduce MobileWorld, a substantially more challenging benchmark designed to reflect real-world usage through 201 tasks across 20 applications. MobileWorld derives its difficulty from an emphasis on long-horizon, cross-application workflows, requiring nearly twice as many completion steps on average (27.8 vs. 14.3) and featuring a significantly higher proportion of multi-app tasks (62.2% vs. 9.5%) than AndroidWorld. To overcome the limitations of existing environments, MobileWorld achieves a balance between production-grade utility and reproducible evaluation by utilizing open-source alternatives to industry standards (e.g., Mattermost for Slack). This approach enables a fully observable and controlled environment through source code modification and direct backend database access for precise verification. MobileWorld also introduces novel task categories, including agent-user interaction and Model Context Protocol (MCP)-augmented tasks, for evaluating agents in user-aware, hybrid-tool scenarios. To facilitate evaluation, we develop a planner-executor agentic framework with extended action spaces to support user interactions and MCP calls. Our results reveal a sharp performance drop compared to AndroidWorld, with the best agentic framework and end-to-end model achieving 51.7% and 20.9% success rates, respectively, highlighting ample headroom for future research.
monitor.why_now: Mobile agents and MCP-style tool use are active areas, and this paper argues the dominant benchmark is already saturating. A harder, more realistic benchmark with cross-app workflows and user interaction is timely for anyone evaluating agent capability claims.
monitor.why_not_higher: This is mainly an evaluation benchmark for a specific mobile-agent setting rather than a paper that changes core model building, training, or serving practice. Its memory/storage relevance is effectively absent, and the episode risks collapsing into a benchmark scoreboard discussion.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Benchmark-heavy rather than system-building impact
Narrower than general LLM infrastructure
Some realism, but still a controlled environment
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Stronger evidence that MobileWorld becomes the standard benchmark and materially changes how top labs evaluate or design agentic systems would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What happens when you stop grading mobile AI agents on an easy exam and force them through messy, multi-app, user-interactive workflows with MCP in the loop?

deferred.title: Exploring Novel Data Storage Approaches for Large-Scale Numerical Weather Prediction
deferred.abstract: Driven by scientific and industry ambition, HPC and AI applications such as operational Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP) require processing and storing ever-increasing data volumes as fast as possible. Whilst POSIX distributed file systems and NVMe SSDs are currently a common HPC storage configuration providing I/O to applications, new storage solutions have proliferated or gained traction over the last decade with potential to address performance limitations POSIX file systems manifest at scale for certain I/O workloads.   This work has primarily aimed to assess the suitability and performance of two object storage systems -namely DAOS and Ceph- for the ECMWF's operational NWP as well as for HPC and AI applications in general. New software-level adapters have been developed which enable the ECMWF's NWP to leverage these systems, and extensive I/O benchmarking has been conducted on a few computer systems, comparing the performance delivered by the evaluated object stores to that of equivalent Lustre file system deployments on the same hardware. Challenges of porting to object storage and its benefits with respect to the traditional POSIX I/O approach have been discussed and, where possible, domain-agnostic performance analysis has been conducted, leading to insight also of relevance to I/O practitioners and the broader HPC community.   DAOS and Ceph have both demonstrated excellent performance, but DAOS stood out relative to Ceph and Lustre, providing superior scalability and flexibility for applications to perform I/O at scale as desired. This sets a promising outlook for DAOS and object storage, which might see greater adoption at HPC centres in the years to come, although not necessarily implying a shift away from POSIX-like I/O.
deferred.why_now: AI and HPC are both running into storage and data-movement bottlenecks, so evidence about object stores versus Lustre on real large-scale workloads is timely. It is useful background for conversations about whether future AI infrastructure should rely less on traditional POSIX file systems.
deferred.why_not_higher: The paper is centered on numerical weather prediction and HPC I/O, not on modern AI training or inference stacks directly. Its main result is a benchmark-driven storage evaluation rather than a change in how most AI teams build or serve models.
downgrade_reasons: HPC/NWP focus rather than mainstream AI workload focus
Storage relevance is adjacent to AI memory systems, not KV-cache/offload/paging core
Limited direct impact on training or inference practice
Episode risks becoming a niche benchmark discussion
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: A stronger direct link to foundation-model training data pipelines, checkpointing, or inference-serving storage bottlenecks would raise it.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the next AI infrastructure bottleneck is not compute or GPU RAM, but the aging POSIX storage stack underneath everything?

deferred.title: Epistemological Fault Lines Between Human and Artificial Intelligence
deferred.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are widely described as artificial intelligence, yet their epistemic profile diverges sharply from human cognition. Here we show that the apparent alignment between human and machine outputs conceals a deeper structural mismatch in how judgments are produced. Tracing the historical shift from symbolic AI and information filtering systems to large-scale generative transformers, we argue that LLMs are not epistemic agents but stochastic pattern-completion systems, formally describable as walks on high-dimensional graphs of linguistic transitions rather than as systems that form beliefs or models of the world. By systematically mapping human and artificial epistemic pipelines, we identify seven epistemic fault lines, divergences in grounding, parsing, experience, motivation, causal reasoning, metacognition, and value. We call the resulting condition Epistemia: a structural situation in which linguistic plausibility substitutes for epistemic evaluation, producing the feeling of knowing without the labor of judgment. We conclude by outlining consequences for evaluation, governance, and epistemic literacy in societies increasingly organized around generative AI.
deferred.why_now: It taps into an active public debate about what LLMs are, how they should be evaluated, and how societies should reason about AI-generated language. The paper gives that debate a clean conceptual frame with its seven fault lines and the notion of 'Epistemia.'
deferred.why_not_higher: It does not present a concrete method, benchmark, or systems result that changes how modern AI is built or served. The memory/storage connection is absent, and the support appears primarily conceptual rather than grounded in realistic model or hardware studies.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Primarily conceptual rather than operational
Weak immediate impact on training, inference, or serving
Limited empirical grounding on realistic workloads
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: A stronger empirical evaluation tying these epistemic fault lines to concrete LLM failure modes, measurement protocols, or deployment decisions would raise it.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: If language models sound like they know, but do not judge, what exactly are we building when plausibility starts to replace understanding?

deferred.title: LegoNet: Memory Footprint Reduction Through Block Weight Clustering
deferred.abstract: As the need for neural network-based applications to become more accurate and powerful grows, so too does their size and memory footprint. With embedded devices, whose cache and RAM are limited, this growth hinders their ability to leverage state-of-the-art neural network architectures. In this work, we propose \textbf{LegoNet}, a compression technique that \textbf{constructs blocks of weights of the entire model regardless of layer type} and clusters these induced blocks. Using blocks instead of individual values to cluster the weights, we were able to compress ResNet-50 trained for Cifar-10 and ImageNet with only 32 4x4 blocks, compressing the memory footprint by over a factor of \textbf{64x without having to remove any weights} or changing the architecture and \textbf{no loss to accuracy}, nor retraining or any data, and show how to find an arrangement of 16 4x4 blocks that gives a compression ratio of \textbf{128x with less than 3\% accuracy loss}. This was all achieved with \textbf{no need for (re)training or fine-tuning}.
deferred.why_now: It targets a real bottleneck: model memory footprint on constrained devices, and it claims unusually large compression without retraining. The no-fine-tuning angle is operationally attractive because it suggests a cheap post-training path to smaller deployments.
deferred.why_not_higher: The evidence is centered on ResNet-50 and image classification rather than modern transformer serving or large-model memory systems. The paper is more about weight compression than first-class AI memory mechanisms like KV cache, offload, paging, or bandwidth scheduling.
downgrade_reasons: vision-centric evaluation
limited relevance to modern transformer workloads
memory link is compression-oriented rather than systems-memory-core
episode risk: mostly a strong benchmark claim
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Show the same block-clustering method on contemporary transformer or LLM workloads with clear memory-bandwidth and latency gains on realistic serving hardware.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if you could shrink a network by 64x to 128x without pruning, retraining, or changing the architecture, and still keep most of the accuracy?

deferred.title: Learning General Policies with Policy Gradient Methods
deferred.abstract: While reinforcement learning methods have delivered remarkable results in a number of settings, generalization, i.e., the ability to produce policies that generalize in a reliable and systematic way, has remained a challenge. The problem of generalization has been addressed formally in classical planning where provable correct policies that generalize over all instances of a given domain have been learned using combinatorial methods. The aim of this work is to bring these two research threads together to illuminate the conditions under which (deep) reinforcement learning approaches, and in particular, policy optimization methods, can be used to learn policies that generalize like combinatorial methods do. We draw on lessons learned from previous combinatorial and deep learning approaches, and extend them in a convenient way. From the former, we model policies as state transition classifiers, as (ground) actions are not general and change from instance to instance. From the latter, we use graph neural networks (GNNs) adapted to deal with relational structures for representing value functions over planning states, and in our case, policies. With these ingredients in place, we find that actor-critic methods can be used to learn policies that generalize almost as well as those obtained using combinatorial approaches while avoiding the scalability bottleneck and the use of feature pools. Moreover, the limitations of the DRL methods on the benchmarks considered have little to do with deep learning or reinforcement learning algorithms, and result from the well-understood expressive limitations of GNNs, and the tradeoff between optimality and generalization (general policies cannot be optimal in some domains). Both of these limitations are addressed without changing the basic DRL methods by adding derived predicates and an alternative cost structure to optimize.
deferred.why_now: It targets a real long-standing problem: learning policies that generalize across instances rather than overfitting fixed tasks. The paper is also a clean attempt to connect classical planning ideas with policy-gradient RL using relational GNN policy representations.
deferred.why_not_higher: The relevance to how people build or serve current AI systems is weak, and the memory/storage connection is absent. The evidence appears centered on planning benchmarks rather than realistic large-model workloads, so transfer to today's AI stack is unclear.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory or storage mechanism
Limited connection to modern LLM training or inference practice
Benchmark-centric evaluation
Mostly planning/RL generalization rather than current systems impact
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: A stronger link to modern agentic or foundation-model planning systems, with evidence on realistic large-model settings, would make it more editorially urgent.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can policy gradients learn the kind of across-instance generalization that classical planners achieved, or do we still need symbolic structure to get truly reusable policies?

deferred.title: The 6th International Verification of Neural Networks Competition (VNN-COMP 2025): Summary and Results
deferred.abstract: This report summarizes the 6th International Verification of Neural Networks Competition (VNN-COMP 2025), held as a part of the 8th International Symposium on AI Verification (SAIV), that was collocated with the 37th International Conference on Computer-Aided Verification (CAV). VNN-COMP is held annually to facilitate the fair and objective comparison of state-of-the-art neural network verification tools, encourage the standardization of tool interfaces, and bring together the neural network verification community. To this end, standardized formats for networks (ONNX) and specification (VNN-LIB) were defined, tools were evaluated on equal-cost hardware (using an automatic evaluation pipeline based on AWS instances), and tool parameters were chosen by the participants before the final test sets were made public. In the 2025 iteration, 8 teams participated on a diverse set of 16 regular and 9 extended benchmarks. This report summarizes the rules, benchmarks, participating tools, results, and lessons learned from this iteration of this competition.
deferred.why_now: It is a useful annual snapshot of the neural network verification field, with standardized benchmarks, tooling, and competition results that help track technical progress. It matters now mainly to researchers working on safety, formal methods, and verification infrastructure.
deferred.why_not_higher: The paper is a competition summary rather than a method that changes mainstream model training, serving, or evaluation practice. Its relevance to current large-scale AI systems is indirect, and the memory/storage connection is effectively absent.
downgrade_reasons: Niche verification audience
No direct memory or storage mechanism
Competition report rather than a deployable method
Limited impact on mainstream LLM build or serve workflows
Episode risk: mostly benchmark recap
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: A stronger link to practical verification of deployed foundation models or concrete implications for real-world model evaluation pipelines would raise priority.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: What does it mean when eight teams race to formally verify neural networks, and why does that still sit at the edge rather than the center of modern AI practice?

deferred.title: Federated Learning Framework for Scalable AI in Heterogeneous HPC and Cloud Environments
deferred.abstract: As the demand grows for scalable and privacy-aware AI systems, Federated Learning (FL) has emerged as a promising solution, allowing decentralized model training without moving raw data. At the same time, the combination of high-performance computing (HPC) and cloud infrastructure offers vast computing power but introduces new complexities, especially when dealing with heterogeneous hardware, communication limits, and non-uniform data. In this work, we present a federated learning framework built to run efficiently across mixed HPC and cloud environments. Our system addresses key challenges such as system heterogeneity, communication overhead, and resource scheduling, while maintaining model accuracy and data privacy. Through experiments on a hybrid testbed, we demonstrate strong performance in terms of scalability, fault tolerance, and convergence, even under non-Independent and Identically Distributed (non-IID) data distributions and varied hardware. These results highlight the potential of federated learning as a practical approach to building scalable Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems in modern, distributed computing settings.
deferred.why_now: Federated learning across mixed HPC and cloud infrastructure is timely as organizations push privacy-aware training beyond a single cluster. The paper also speaks to real deployment friction around heterogeneity, communication limits, and scheduling in distributed AI systems.
deferred.why_not_higher: The memory/storage link is mostly indirect: this is a distributed training orchestration paper, not a concrete memory, cache, paging, or offload paper. The abstract also reads like a framework-and-benchmark result without enough detail on model scale, workloads, or hardware realism to make it feel important to the broader AI audience right now.
downgrade_reasons: Memory connection is adjacent rather than core
Federated learning remains niche for the main podcast audience
Abstract lacks concrete large-model or production-scale evidence
Episode would risk becoming a general benchmark discussion
