deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can federated learning actually scale across messy real-world mixes of supercomputers and cloud, or is this still more orchestration demo than a new blueprint for modern AI?

deferred.title: HAWX: A Hardware-Aware FrameWork for Fast and Scalable ApproXimation of DNNs
deferred.abstract: This work presents HAWX, a hardware-aware scalable exploration framework that employs multi-level sensitivity scoring at different DNN abstraction levels (operator, filter, layer, and model) to guide selective integration of heterogeneous AxC blocks. Supported by predictive models for accuracy, power, and area, HAWX accelerates the evaluation of candidate configurations, achieving over 23* speedup in a layer-level search with two candidate approximate blocks and more than (3*106)* speedup at the filter-level search only for LeNet-5, while maintaining accuracy comparable to exhaustive search. Experiments across state-of-the-art DNN benchmarks such as VGG-11, ResNet-18, and EfficientNetLite demonstrate that the efficiency benefits of HAWX scale exponentially with network size. The HAWX hardware-aware search algorithm supports both spatial and temporal accelerator architectures, leveraging either off-the-shelf approximate components or customized designs.
deferred.why_now: Hardware-aware approximation remains relevant as edge and accelerator designers keep looking for better power, area, and latency tradeoffs in DNN inference. The paper offers a fast search framework for exploring approximate-computing design choices rather than brute-forcing them.
deferred.why_not_higher: The paper is not tightly connected to current mainstream AI workflows such as LLM training, serving, or memory bottlenecks. Its evidence is centered on vision benchmarks and approximate hardware blocks, which makes the transfer to modern frontier-model practice indirect.
downgrade_reasons: memory/storage link is absent
limited relevance to modern foundation-model pipelines
benchmark-heavy hardware-search framing
unclear impact on current production AI systems
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: A direct demonstration on modern transformer inference or training hardware with clear bandwidth, cache, or serving gains would raise it.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if you could search millions of neural hardware approximation choices in minutes and keep nearly the same accuracy without exhaustive design-space exploration?

deferred.title: GAPSL: A Gradient-Aligned Parallel Split Learning on Heterogeneous Data
deferred.abstract: The increasing complexity of neural networks poses significant challenges for democratizing FL on resource?constrained client devices. Parallel split learning (PSL) has emerged as a promising solution by offloading substantial computing workload to a server via model partitioning, shrinking client-side computing load, and eliminating the client-side model aggregation for reduced communication and deployment costs. Since PSL is aggregation-free, it suffers from severe training divergence stemming from gradient directional inconsistency across clients. To address this challenge, we propose GAPSL, a gradient-aligned PSL framework that comprises two key components: leader gradient identification (LGI) and gradient direction alignment (GDA). LGI dynamically selects a set of directionally consistent client gradients to construct a leader gradient that captures the global convergence trend. GDA employs a direction-aware regularization to align each client's gradient with the leader gradient, thereby mitigating inter-device gradient directional inconsistency and enhancing model convergence. We evaluate GAPSL on a prototype computing testbed. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GAPSL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art benchmarks in training accuracy and latency.
deferred.why_now: Split learning is relevant for pushing training onto weaker edge devices, and this paper targets a real pain point: divergence under heterogeneous clients. It matters now mainly as part of the broader interest in decentralized and resource-constrained AI training.
deferred.why_not_higher: The memory/storage link is weak because the core contribution is gradient alignment for convergence, not cache, bandwidth, paging, offload, or data movement behavior. It also feels like a niche distributed training optimization rather than something that changes mainstream AI system design in the next 30-90 days.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage mechanism is absent rather than central
Federated/split learning remains a niche topic for the podcast's broad audience
Impact is mostly on a specific training setup, not general AI practice
Episode risk: becomes a benchmark-and-method paper without broader payoff
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: A stronger case would show clear gains on modern large-model workloads or edge deployment settings where communication, memory footprint, and latency constraints materially change system design decisions.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can split learning actually work on messy, heterogeneous edge devices, or do client gradients tear training apart before the model converges?

deferred.title: On the Structure of Stationary Solutions to McKean-Vlasov Equations with Applications to Noisy Transformers
deferred.abstract: We study stationary solutions of McKean-Vlasov equations on the circle. Our main contributions stem from observing an exact equivalence between solutions of the stationary McKean-Vlasov equation and an infinite-dimensional quadratic system of equations over Fourier coefficients, which allows explicit characterization of the stationary states in a sequence space rather than a function space. This framework provides a transparent description of local bifurcations, characterizing their periodicity, and resonance structures, while accommodating singular potentials. We derive analytic expressions that characterize the emergence, form and shape (supercritical, critical, subcritical or transcritical) of bifurcations involving possibly multiple Fourier modes and connect them with discontinuous phase transitions. We also characterize, under suitable assumptions, the detailed structure of the stationary bifurcating solutions that are accurate upto an arbitrary number of Fourier modes. At the global level, we establish regularity and concavity properties of the free energy landscape, proving existence, compactness, and coexistence of globally minimizing stationary measures, further identifying discontinuous phase transitions with points of non-differentiability of the minimum free energy map. As an application, we specialize the theory to the Noisy Mean-Field Transformer model, where we show how changing the inverse temperature parameter $β$ affects the geometry of the infinitely many bifurcations from the uniform measure. We also explain how increasing $β$ can lead to a rich class of approximate multi-mode stationary solutions which can be seen as `metastable states'. Further, a sharp transition from continuous to discontinuous (first-order) phase behavior is observed as $β$ increases.
deferred.why_now: It is a serious mathematical analysis of noisy mean-field transformer dynamics and phase transitions, which could matter if the field starts using this framework to reason about transformer behavior under noise and temperature-like control parameters. The paper is timely as theory around transformer dynamics continues to expand, but its immediate relevance is mostly to specialist readers.
deferred.why_not_higher: The work does not clearly change how practitioners build, train, serve, or evaluate modern AI systems in the next 30-90 days. Its memory/storage relevance is effectively absent, and the evidence is analytic rather than grounded in realistic LLM workloads, serving systems, or hardware behavior.
downgrade_reasons: Theory-heavy with limited near-term practitioner impact
No direct memory, cache, bandwidth, offload, or storage mechanism
Transformer connection is mean-field and abstract rather than systems-realistic
Lacks empirical validation on modern large-model workloads or hardware
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Concrete demonstrations that the phase behavior predicts or improves training/inference behavior in realistic transformer models would raise priority.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if transformer behavior under noise can be mapped to a phase-transition story, with hidden metastable states emerging from an exact Fourier-space description?

out_of_scope.title: UltraHR-100K: Enhancing UHR Image Synthesis with A Large-Scale High-Quality Dataset
out_of_scope.abstract: Ultra-high-resolution (UHR) text-to-image (T2I) generation has seen notable progress. However, two key challenges remain : 1) the absence of a large-scale high-quality UHR T2I dataset, and (2) the neglect of tailored training strategies for fine-grained detail synthesis in UHR scenarios. To tackle the first challenge, we introduce \textbf{UltraHR-100K}, a high-quality dataset of 100K UHR images with rich captions, offering diverse content and strong visual fidelity. Each image exceeds 3K resolution and is rigorously curated based on detail richness, content complexity, and aesthetic quality. To tackle the second challenge, we propose a frequency-aware post-training method that enhances fine-detail generation in T2I diffusion models. Specifically, we design (i) \textit{Detail-Oriented Timestep Sampling (DOTS)} to focus learning on detail-critical denoising steps, and (ii) \textit{Soft-Weighting Frequency Regularization (SWFR)}, which leverages Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) to softly constrain frequency components, encouraging high-frequency detail preservation. Extensive experiments on our proposed UltraHR-eval4K benchmarks demonstrate that our approach significantly improves the fine-grained detail quality and overall fidelity of UHR image generation. The code is available at \href{https://github.com/NJU-PCALab/UltraHR-100k}{here}.
out_of_scope.why_now: Ultra-high-resolution image generation is an active niche in generative vision, and a 100K curated 3K+ dataset plus detail-focused post-training could matter to teams pushing premium visual fidelity. It is timely mainly because image generation quality races continue to reward better data curation and fine-detail training recipes.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This is still a narrow text-to-image vision paper centered on dataset quality and benchmark gains, not a paper that changes mainstream AI system design or operations. The memory/storage link is effectively absent, and the method does not obviously transfer beyond UHR diffusion-style image synthesis.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow UHR text-to-image task
Memory/storage relevance absent
Primarily dataset and benchmark driven
Limited impact on mainstream training/serving practice
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: Show that the frequency-aware training recipe reliably improves multiple generative model families or materially changes production image-generation pipelines at scale.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: What does it actually take to make diffusion models produce crisp 4K details instead of just bigger blurry images?

out_of_scope.title: IB-GAN: Disentangled Representation Learning with Information Bottleneck Generative Adversarial Networks
out_of_scope.abstract: We propose a new GAN-based unsupervised model for disentangled representation learning. The new model is discovered in an attempt to utilize the Information Bottleneck (IB) framework to the optimization of GAN, thereby named IB-GAN. The architecture of IB-GAN is partially similar to that of InfoGAN but has a critical difference; an intermediate layer of the generator is leveraged to constrain the mutual information between the input and the generated output. The intermediate stochastic layer can serve as a learnable latent distribution that is trained with the generator jointly in an end-to-end fashion. As a result, the generator of IB-GAN can harness the latent space in a disentangled and interpretable manner. With the experiments on dSprites and Color-dSprites dataset, we demonstrate that IB-GAN achieves competitive disentanglement scores to those of state-of-the-art \b{eta}-VAEs and outperforms InfoGAN. Moreover, the visual quality and the diversity of samples generated by IB-GAN are often better than those by \b{eta}-VAEs and Info-GAN in terms of FID score on CelebA and 3D Chairs dataset.
out_of_scope.why_now: It touches a durable idea in generative modeling: learning controllable and interpretable latent factors. That said, it does not connect strongly to the current 30-90 day conversation around modern foundation model training, inference, or systems.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: The work is centered on GAN disentanglement benchmarks rather than methods that change how current AI systems are built or served. Its evaluations are on small vision datasets and classic generative setups, not realistic modern model workloads.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Limited relevance to modern AI system design
Benchmark-driven on narrow generative vision tasks
Weak evidence on realistic large-scale workloads
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: A clear link to modern latent-control methods in large multimodal or diffusion/LLM systems, with evidence that the approach improves real deployment or training behavior, would raise it.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can an information bottleneck make GAN latents genuinely disentangled, or is this just another neat benchmark win from a pre-foundation-model era?

out_of_scope.title: Algerian Dialect
out_of_scope.abstract: We present Algerian Dialect, a large-scale sentiment-annotated dataset consisting of 45,000 YouTube comments written in Algerian Arabic dialect. The comments were collected from more than 30 Algerian press and media channels using the YouTube Data API. Each comment is manually annotated into one of five sentiment categories: very negative, negative, neutral, positive, and very positive. In addition to sentiment labels, the dataset includes rich metadata such as collection timestamps, like counts, video URLs, and annotation dates. This dataset addresses the scarcity of publicly available resources for Algerian dialect and aims to support research in sentiment analysis, dialectal Arabic NLP, and social media analytics. The dataset is publicly available on Mendeley Data under a CC BY 4.0 license at https://doi.org/10.17632/zzwg3nnhsz.2.
out_of_scope.why_now: Low-resource dialect NLP datasets remain useful for benchmarking sentiment models and multilingual evaluation. A 45,000-comment, manually labeled Algerian Arabic resource could help researchers working on Arabic dialect coverage and social media NLP.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This is a narrow dataset release rather than a paper that changes how modern AI systems are built, trained, served, or evaluated at large. It has no direct memory/storage angle and limited transfer beyond dialectal sentiment analysis.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow task dataset paper
No direct memory/storage mechanism
Limited systems relevance
Episode would likely be benchmark-and-resource focused
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: Stronger priority would come from a broadly reusable method for low-resource dialect modeling or evaluation that clearly transfers across multilingual LLM training or deployment.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: What does it take to build a serious sentiment benchmark for a low-resource Arabic dialect, and why do these datasets still shape what multilingual AI can actually understand?

out_of_scope.title: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Optimal Portfolio Allocation: A Comparative Study with Mean-Variance Optimization
out_of_scope.abstract: Portfolio Management is the process of overseeing a group of investments, referred to as a portfolio, with the objective of achieving predetermined investment goals. Portfolio optimization is a key component that involves allocating the portfolio assets so as to maximize returns while minimizing risk taken. It is typically carried out by financial professionals who use a combination of quantitative techniques and investment expertise to make decisions about the portfolio allocation.   Recent applications of Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) have shown promising results when used to optimize portfolio allocation by training model-free agents on historical market data. Many of these methods compare their results against basic benchmarks or other state-of-the-art DRL agents but often fail to compare their performance against traditional methods used by financial professionals in practical settings. One of the most commonly used methods for this task is Mean-Variance Portfolio Optimization (MVO), which uses historical time series information to estimate expected asset returns and covariances, which are then used to optimize for an investment objective.   Our work is a thorough comparison between model-free DRL and MVO for optimal portfolio allocation. We detail the specifics of how to make DRL for portfolio optimization work in practice, also noting the adjustments needed for MVO. Backtest results demonstrate strong performance of the DRL agent across many metrics, including Sharpe ratio, maximum drawdowns, and absolute returns.
out_of_scope.why_now: It is a practical comparison of deep reinforcement learning against a standard finance baseline, which may interest a small slice of ML-for-finance readers. The benchmarking discipline is more useful than many application papers, but the core contribution stays within portfolio allocation.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This does not clearly affect how the broader AI field builds, trains, serves, or evaluates modern AI systems, and the memory/storage connection is absent. The paper is also a narrow domain task rather than a generally transferable AI systems or methods result.
downgrade_reasons: narrow finance application
no direct memory/storage mechanism
limited relevance to modern AI system design
benchmark-driven rather than field-shifting
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: Stronger evidence that its DRL methodology or evaluation lessons transfer to general sequential decision-making problems used in mainstream AI practice would raise priority.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: What happens when deep reinforcement learning goes head-to-head with classic mean-variance optimization in the one domain where ML hype meets hard risk constraints: portfolio allocation?

out_of_scope.title: Bonsai: A Framework for Convolutional Neural Network Acceleration Using Criterion-Based Pruning
out_of_scope.abstract: As the need for more accurate and powerful Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) increases, so too does the size, execution time, memory footprint, and power consumption. To overcome this, solutions such as pruning have been proposed with their own metrics and methodologies, or criteria, for how weights should be removed. These solutions do not share a common implementation and are difficult to implement and compare. In this work, we introduce Combine, a criterion- based pruning solution and demonstrate that it is fast and effective framework for iterative pruning, demonstrate that criterion have differing effects on different models, create a standard language for comparing criterion functions, and propose a few novel criterion functions. We show the capacity of these criterion functions and the framework on VGG inspired models, pruning up to 79\% of filters while retaining or improving accuracy, and reducing the computations needed by the network by up to 68\%.
out_of_scope.why_now: Model efficiency still matters, and pruning remains a standard compression technique for constrained deployments. A framework that standardizes criterion-based pruning could be useful for practitioners comparing pruning rules in legacy CNN settings.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: The paper is centered on CNN pruning with VGG-style models rather than the transformer, LLM, or serving stacks driving current AI attention. Its memory relevance is indirect, and the evidence does not appear to cover realistic modern models, hardware, or deployment regimes.
downgrade_reasons: CNN/VGG-focused rather than modern frontier AI workloads
Memory benefit is indirect model compression, not a concrete cache/offload/bandwidth mechanism
Evaluation appears narrow and not clearly on realistic production hardware
Episode value is mostly benchmark-and-framework comparison rather than a shift in practice
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: Show strong results on modern transformer or multimodal models with clear deployment wins in latency, bandwidth, or memory capacity on real inference hardware.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: Pruning can still shrink old-school vision models, but this paper mainly asks whether better pruning criteria matter enough to revive CNN efficiency as a current AI story.

out_of_scope.title: A Fundamental Algorithm for Dependency Parsing (With Corrections)
out_of_scope.abstract: This paper presents a fundamental algorithm for parsing natural language sentences into dependency trees. Unlike phrase-structure (constituency) parsers, this algorithm operates one word at a time, attaching each word as soon as it can be attached, corresponding to properties claimed for the parser in the human brain. Like phrase-structure parsing, its worst-case complexity is $O(n^3)$, but in human language, the worst case occurs only for small $n$.
out_of_scope.why_now: It is a historical dependency parsing algorithm, not a paper that shifts current practice in foundation models or AI systems. Its relevance today is mainly archival or educational within classical NLP.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: The work does not connect directly to modern LLM training, inference, evaluation, or memory/storage systems. It is also centered on dependency parsing rather than a broadly transferable systems or model-building advance.
downgrade_reasons: historical classical NLP paper
no direct memory/storage mechanism
little relevance to current LLM build/train/serve practice
limited 30-90 day audience urgency
episode would skew retrospective rather than actionable
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: A concrete link to modern structured decoding, retrieval, or efficient long-context language modeling would make it more relevant.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: Before transformers dominated NLP, could a word-by-word dependency parser tell us anything useful about how language structure should be built incrementally?

out_of_scope.title: Consistency-guided semi-supervised outlier detection in heterogeneous data using fuzzy rough sets
out_of_scope.abstract: Outlier detection aims to find samples that behave differently from the majority of the data. Semi-supervised detection methods can utilize the supervision of partial labels, thus reducing false positive rates. However, most of the current semi-supervised methods focus on numerical data and neglect the heterogeneity of data information. In this paper, we propose a consistency-guided outlier detection algorithm (COD) for heterogeneous data with the fuzzy rough set theory in a semi-supervised manner. First, a few labeled outliers are leveraged to construct label-informed fuzzy similarity relations. Next, the consistency of the fuzzy decision system is introduced to evaluate attributes' contributions to knowledge classification. Subsequently, we define the outlier factor based on the fuzzy similarity class and predict outliers by integrating the classification consistency and the outlier factor. The proposed algorithm is extensively evaluated on 15 freshly proposed datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that COD is better than or comparable with the leading outlier detectors. This manuscript is the accepted author version of a paper published by Elsevier. The final published version is available at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.asoc.2024.112070
out_of_scope.why_now: It is a recent semi-supervised outlier detection paper for heterogeneous data, which may matter to specialists working on anomaly detection with mixed data types. The accepted-journal status and fresh datasets give it some topical legitimacy within that subarea.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This does not appear to change how the broader AI field builds, trains, serves, or evaluates modern foundation-model systems. The method is a narrow tabular/heterogeneous-data detection approach with no direct memory, storage, or large-model systems relevance.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow task paper rather than a broadly consequential AI systems paper
No direct memory or storage mechanism
Weak connection to modern foundation-model practice
Benchmark-driven evaluation on datasets, not realistic large-scale AI workloads or hardware
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: A clear demonstration that the consistency-guided fuzzy rough set approach materially improves anomaly detection in production-scale AI pipelines or transfers to modern model monitoring and data quality systems would raise priority.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can a fuzzy-rough, label-guided notion of consistency make anomaly detection on messy heterogeneous data actually more reliable, or is this mainly a niche benchmark gain?

out_of_scope.title: FLAS: a combination of proactive and reactive auto-scaling architecture for distributed services
out_of_scope.abstract: Cloud computing has established itself as the support for the vast majority of emerging technologies, mainly due to the characteristic of elasticity it offers. Auto-scalers are the systems that enable this elasticity by acquiring and releasing resources on demand to ensure an agreed service level. In this article we present FLAS (Forecasted Load Auto-Scaling), an auto-scaler for distributed services that combines the advantages of proactive and reactive approaches according to the situation to decide the optimal scaling actions in every moment. The main novelties introduced by FLAS are (i) a predictive model of the high-level metrics trend which allows to anticipate changes in the relevant SLA parameters (e.g. performance metrics such as response time or throughput) and (ii) a reactive contingency system based on the estimation of high-level metrics from resource use metrics, reducing the necessary instrumentation (less invasive) and allowing it to be adapted agnostically to different applications. We provide a FLAS implementation for the use case of a content-based publish-subscribe middleware (E-SilboPS) that is the cornerstone of an event-driven architecture. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first auto-scaling system for content-based publish-subscribe distributed systems (although it is generic enough to fit any distributed service). Through an evaluation based on several test cases recreating not only the expected contexts of use, but also the worst possible scenarios (following the Boundary-Value Analysis or BVA test methodology), we have validated our approach and demonstrated the effectiveness of our solution by ensuring compliance with performance requirements over 99% of the time.
out_of_scope.why_now: Elastic autoscaling is an important systems problem, especially for cloud services under bursty demand. The proactive-plus-reactive control idea is sensible, but it is not meaningfully tied to current AI training or inference bottlenecks.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This is a distributed services autoscaling paper, not an AI systems paper in the editorial sense used by the show. Its memory/storage relevance is effectively absent, and the evaluation is on a publish-subscribe middleware rather than modern model-serving workloads or accelerator stacks.
downgrade_reasons: Not directly about modern AI system design or evaluation
Memory/storage link is absent rather than concrete
Validation workload is narrow and non-AI
Episode would likely collapse into autoscaling benchmark details
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: A replication on LLM serving or training infrastructure with concrete effects on GPU utilization, KV cache pressure, memory tiering, or inference latency would raise it sharply.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can a hybrid predictive-and-reactive autoscaler keep distributed services inside SLA limits, and what breaks when you try to carry that idea into real AI infrastructure?

out_of_scope.title: DistributedEstimator: Distributed Training of Quantum Neural Networks via Circuit Cutting
out_of_scope.abstract: Circuit cutting decomposes a large quantum circuit into a collection of smaller subcircuits. The outputs of these subcircuits are then classically reconstructed to recover the original expectation values. While prior work characterises cutting overhead largely in terms of subcircuit counts and sampling complexity, its end-to-end impact on iterative, estimator-driven training pipelines remains insufficiently measured from a systems perspective. In this paper, we propose a cut-aware estimator execution pipeline that treats circuit cutting as a staged distributed workload and instruments each estimator query into partitioning, subexperiment generation, parallel execution, and classical reconstruction phases. Using logged runtime traces and learning outcomes on two binary classification workloads (Iris and MNIST), we quantify cutting overheads, scaling limits, and sensitivity to injected stragglers, and we evaluate whether accuracy and robustness are preserved under matched training budgets. Our measurements show that cutting introduces substantial end-to-end overheads that grow with the number of cuts, and that reconstruction constitutes a dominant fraction of per-query time, bounding achievable speed-up under increased parallelism. Despite these systems costs, test accuracy and robustness are preserved in the measured regimes, with configuration-dependent improvements observed in some cut settings. These results indicate that practical scaling of circuit cutting for learning workloads hinges on reducing and overlapping reconstruction and on scheduling policies that account for barrier-dominated critical paths.
out_of_scope.why_now: It is a useful systems-style measurement paper for quantum ML practitioners because it quantifies where circuit cutting training time actually goes and shows reconstruction/barrier costs as the scaling bottleneck. That said, its relevance is mostly confined to the current quantum neural network tooling niche rather than mainstream AI practice.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: The experiments are built on small binary classification workloads and a quantum-specific training setup, so the results do not transfer cleanly to how most AI researchers build or serve models today. The paper studies distributed execution overheads, but not memory/storage mechanisms central to the podcast's second lens.
downgrade_reasons: quantum ML niche rather than mainstream AI systems
memory/storage link is absent, not core or adjacent in the podcast sense
evaluation uses small tasks like Iris and MNIST
limited transfer to contemporary large-model training or inference practice
better as a niche benchmark discussion than a strong general-interest episode
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: A stronger case would include large-scale workloads and explicit lessons that transfer to mainstream distributed AI training, such as scheduler or systems techniques applicable beyond quantum circuits.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: What happens when you treat circuit cutting not as quantum theory, but as a distributed systems problem with stragglers, barriers, and reconstruction dominating the bill?

out_of_scope.title: InstructNet: A Novel Approach for Multi-Label Instruction Classification through Advanced Deep Learning
out_of_scope.abstract: People use search engines for various topics and items, from daily essentials to more aspirational and specialized objects. Therefore, search engines have taken over as peoples preferred resource. The How To prefix has become familiar and widely used in various search styles to find solutions to particular problems. This search allows people to find sequential instructions by providing detailed guidelines to accomplish specific tasks. Categorizing instructional text is also essential for task-oriented learning and creating knowledge bases. This study uses the How To articles to determine the multi-label instruction category. We have brought this work with a dataset comprising 11,121 observations from wikiHow, where each record has multiple categories. To find out the multi-label category meticulously, we employ some transformer-based deep neural architectures, such as Generalized Autoregressive Pretraining for Language Understanding (XLNet), Bidirectional Encoder Representation from Transformers (BERT), etc. In our multi-label instruction classification process, we have reckoned our proposed architectures using accuracy and macro f1-score as the performance metrics. This thorough evaluation showed us much about our strategys strengths and drawbacks. Specifically, our implementation of the XLNet architecture has demonstrated unprecedented performance, achieving an accuracy of 97.30% and micro and macro average scores of 89.02% and 93%, a noteworthy accomplishment in multi-label classification. This high level of accuracy and macro average score is a testament to the effectiveness of the XLNet architecture in our proposed InstructNet approach. By employing a multi-level strategy in our evaluation process, we have gained a more comprehensive knowledge of the effectiveness of our proposed architectures and identified areas for forthcoming improvement and refinement.
out_of_scope.why_now: Instruction understanding for search and how-to content is a legitimate application area, but this paper does not address a live shift in mainstream AI practice or infrastructure. Its main contribution is a task-specific multi-label classification result on wikiHow-style data.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This is a narrow application paper built around standard transformer classifiers rather than a method that changes how modern AI systems are built, served, or evaluated. The memory/storage connection is absent, and the reported gains appear confined to a bespoke benchmark setup.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow task-specific classification paper
No direct memory or storage mechanism
Limited systems relevance to current LLM practice
Benchmark-focused result with unclear transfer to broader AI workflows
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: Show that the method transfers to modern instruction-tuned LLM pipelines or large-scale retrieval/search systems with clear operational gains.

out_of_scope.title: PIM-FW: Hardware-Software Co-Design of All-pairs Shortest Paths in DRAM
out_of_scope.abstract: All-pairs shortest paths (APSP) is a fundamental algorithm used for routing, logistics, and network analysis, but the cubic time complexity and heavy data movement of the canonical Floyd-Warshall (FW) algorithm severely limits its scalability on conventional CPUs or GPUs. In this paper, we propose PIM-FW, a novel co-designed hardware architecture and dataflow that leverages processing in and near memory architecture designed to accelerate blocked FW algorithm on an HBM3 stack. To enable fine-grained parallelism, we propose a massively parallel array of specialized bit-serial bank PE and channel PE designed to accelerate the core min-plus operations. Our novel dataflow complements this hardware, employing an interleaved mapping policy for superior load balancing and hybrid in and near memory computing model for efficient computation and reduction. The novel in-bank computing approach allows all distance updates to be performed and stored in memory bank, a key contribution is that eliminates the data movement bottleneck inherent in GPU-based approaches. We implement a full software and hardware co-design using a cycle-accurate simulator to simulate an 8-channel, 4-Hi HBM3 PIM stack on real road-network traces. Experimental results show that, for a 8192 x 8192 graph, PIM-FW achieves a 18.7x speedup in end-to-end execution, and consumes 3200x less DRAM energy compared to a state-of-the-art GPU-only Floyd-Warshall.
out_of_scope.why_now: It is a serious memory-centric hardware paper about reducing data movement with in/near-memory compute on HBM3, which is adjacent to current interest in bandwidth-bound AI systems. The co-design angle is timely because AI accelerators are also running into memory wall constraints.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: The paper is not actually about modern AI workloads, models, training, or inference; it accelerates all-pairs shortest paths on road-network traces. Its memory story is architectural but not tied to KV cache, offload, paging, serving, or any concrete large-model systems behavior.
downgrade_reasons: Not an AI workload paper
Memory relevance is adjacent rather than core to AI
Evaluation is on graph workloads, not model training or inference
Transfer to transformer systems is speculative
Primarily a benchmarked domain accelerator story
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: Show the same PIM/HBM co-design principles accelerating real LLM or recommender memory bottlenecks with concrete wins on serving or training workloads.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the right answer to the memory wall is to move shortest-path computation into HBM itself, and what does that teach us about where AI accelerators may go next?
